This is one of the most celebrated specialist pairings in modern audio. Let
me unpack what each piece is doing and why the combination is so well
regarded.

The Denafrips Pontus II is a discrete R2R ladder DAC. Unlike chip-based
delta-sigma designs that achieve their numbers through aggressive
oversampling and noise shaping, the Pontus converts each sample with a
discrete resistor network. The sonic consequence is greater tonal density,
harmonic texture, and what reviewers tend to call "musical flow." It gives
up some last-degree microdetail and some imaging precision compared to a
top delta-sigma DAC, but it produces an analogue-like body that's hard to
walk away from once you've heard it.

The Leben CS600X is a push-pull tube integrated amplifier — about 32 watts
into 8 ohms, with KT77 / KT88 / EL34 output tube options that meaningfully
change the voicing. It's not the most powerful amp, and it's not the most
"accurate" amp by measurement standards, but it has been refined over
generations and the chassis is mature. The CS600X is what audiophiles
mean when they say "voiced by an engineer who listens." Tonal density is
real; transient drive is present; there's an unforced quality to dynamics
that smaller solid-state designs struggle to match.

The DeVore O/96 is John DeVore's flagship Orangutan — a wide-baffle,
high-sensitivity (around 96 dB), full-range two-way design that was
specifically built to be driven by low-to-moderate-power tube amplifiers
exactly like the Leben. The cabinet width is unusual on purpose; the
voicing is warm, rhythmically alive, and emotionally direct. DeVore makes
no measurement claims for the O/96; he makes musical ones.

Together this is a coherent system in the strongest sense of the term —
not three components that happen to work, but three pieces whose design
priorities are aligned around the same outcome. Harmonic density carries
through from the R2R source, through the tube amp's even-order character,
into the DeVore's tonally dense midrange and unforced dynamic envelope.
Nothing in the chain is "fighting" anything else. The 32 watts of the
CS600X are more than enough for the 96 dB sensitivity of the O/96 in any
realistic room, which means the amp stays well within its comfortable
operating region — and you get the elasticity and ease that comes from an
amplifier that's never near clipping.

The trade-off, if there is one, is purely a question of taste. This chain
is unapologetically warm, dense, and rhythmically present. Brighter or
more analytically-recorded material may sound less spotlit at the top —
that's not a failure, it's the voicing. If you wanted more upper-band
extension and air, you'd be looking at a different system architecture
entirely, not a tweak. The Pontus, the Leben, and the O/96 have committed
to a single coherent philosophy, and they reward the commitment.

For most listeners this is exactly the kind of chain that gets played for
hours rather than evaluated for minutes. It rewards familiar recordings
and pulls you back to the music. The components are also long-term-keepable
— none of them is fragile to technology drift; all three will sound the
same in ten years as they do today.
